driver.body: Supply and demand do not clear themselves. The matching engine is the **crankshaft** of the logistics marketplace — the code that, every few seconds, scans every open order and every idle courier inside a cell and decides who gets what [S-042]. Its objective function is not raw delivery speed but the **liquidity composite**: the engine is scored on the health of the market it is clearing, not on the convenience of any single order. That distinction is the entire design. A naive nearest-courier matcher looks fine at low load and collapses under surge, because greedy local matches strand the distant orders no idle courier is ever offered — so the engine has to optimize the market as a system, not each order in isolation [S-042].

mechanisms.body:
On each short dispatch tick the engine does not match orders one at a time. It **batches** every open order and every idle courier in the cell into a single bipartite assignment problem, then solves it for a blended cost — ETA, batching gain, courier fairness, and predicted acceptance — so the whole cell clears together rather than order-by-order [S-043]. The pseudocode is the actual shape of the production loop, not an illustration of it [S-311].

Four cross-feeds are load-bearing inside that one function, and each is a different domain wired straight into the matcher:

- **D1_travel_time** replaces straight-line distance with congestion-real ETA — the single biggest match-quality gain, worth **+9pts of fill rate** in dense cells [S-312].
- **D6_accept_model** predicts, per courier per batch, the probability of acceptance, so each job is offered to the courier most likely to take it and do it well — cutting the decline-and-reoffer churn that otherwise adds **40–90s of latency** to an order [S-043].
- **D2_energy_ok** refuses to hand a courier a batch their remaining charge cannot finish, so an accepted job is a completable one [S-043].
- **fairness_debt** protects the professional core's earnings from being starved by the optimizer — the term a standalone matcher lacks, and the reason our core churns at **1.1% not 6%** [S-043].

Strip out any one of the four and the objective stops describing the market: the batch is precisely what lets ETA, acceptance, energy, and fairness be priced against each other in a single solve rather than fought over one greedy match at a time [S-043].

The matcher is where the flywheel compounds **fastest**, because the engine's own output is its next input: every completed offer and every declined offer is a labeled training example fed straight back to D6 [S-044]. Match quality this quarter trains the acceptance model that raises match quality next quarter — a closed loop, not a one-time gain [S-313].

This is the mechanism by which **L stops being a subsidy we buy and becomes a moat we own**. A competitor can match our courier pay for a week, but cannot match a model trained on **340 million dispatch decisions per month** across six correlated domains — the data is the asset, and it is not for sale [S-044]. The proof is in the spread: the gap between our fill rate and a well-funded standalone's has widened from **4pts to 11pts over eight quarters** at equal courier pay — not because we spend more, but because the engine has learned more [S-314].
